attributes.stem:
A 51-year-old man is admitted to the Intensive Care Unit with septic shock requiring mechanical ventilation. He has metastatic non-small cell lung cancer and is currently receiving combination chemotherapy and immunotherapy.

His medical history includes chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and previous left lower lobectomy. He has had a prolonged admission with multiple complications.

After extensive discussion about his poor prognosis, his wife has decided to withdraw life-sustaining therapy, understanding he is likely to die. She notes that he is a registered organ donor and asks about the possibility of organ donation after his death.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following statements is most accurate regarding organ donation in this case?
options: He can be considered for donation after circulatory death once sepsis resolves
He is ineligible for organ donation due to active disseminated malignancy
He is eligible for donation after brain death if this occurs prior to withdrawal
He can donate corneas and heart valves despite having metastatic cancer
He is ineligible for organ donation due to recent chemo-immunotherapy dosage
attributes.explanation:
This case highlights important considerations in organ donation, particularly regarding contraindications and eligibility criteria. He is ineligible for organ donation due to active disseminated malignancy is the correct answer as the Transplantation Society of Australia and New Zealand (TSANZ) guidelines specify active malignancy as an absolute contraindication to organ donation, regardless of organ function.

The suggestion that he can be considered for donation after circulatory death once sepsis resolves is incorrect. While sepsis alone might not permanently exclude donation if completely resolved, the presence of active malignancy remains an absolute contraindication.

He is eligible for donation after brain death if this occurs prior to withdrawal is incorrect. The pathway to donation (whether circulatory death or brain death) does not affect the contraindication of active malignancy.

He can donate corneas and heart valves despite having metastatic cancer is incorrect. While some tissue donations might be possible with certain types of cancer, metastatic cancer remains a contraindication for both organ and tissue donation.

He is ineligible for organ donation due to recent chemo-immunotherapy dosage is incorrect. While recent chemotherapy and immunotherapy might affect organ function and could be a relative contraindication, it is not the primary reason for ineligibility in this case. The presence of active metastatic malignancy is the absolute contraindication that makes all other considerations irrelevant.

The rationale behind excluding active malignancy is the risk of transmitting cancer to immunosuppressed transplant recipients. There have been documented cases of donor-transmitted malignancies, which can be rapidly progressive in immunosuppressed recipients. This risk is considered unacceptable, leading to the absolute contraindication in current guidelines.

TSANZ guidelines do stratify risks for donor transmitted malignancy and recommendations based on both donor and recipient factors, which can allow in some cases organ utilisation in patients with distant or treated cancer.
learningPoints: Active malignancy is an absolute contraindication to organ donation according to current Transplantation Society of Australia and New Zealand guidelines.
Donation after circulatory death requires careful timing and coordination between withdrawal of life support and organ retrieval.
The presence of sepsis alone is not an absolute contraindication to organ donation, but active malignancy is.
references: Clinical Guidelines for Organ Transplantation from Deceased Donors. The Transplantation Society of Australia and New Zealand. 2024 August. Available online at ; https://tsanz.com.au/clinical-guidelines

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
